title: A Look at Moon Towers, an Expansion for Paupers' Ladder
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rixzhpBKdfg

Daniel (@dungeondive) unboxes Moon Towers, the first substantial expansion for Pauper's Ladder — and suspects it's one of the better ones, adding over 400 cards. Mysterious bone-white towers have risen across Brighthelm (the noble knights, naturally, are too busy to deal with them), so it falls to the paupers. Crucially, Moon Towers is mostly kept *separate* from the base game — a fresh adventure rather than a dilution — though he notes the equipment decks could happily be combined.

He tours the haul: new region encounter decks, quests, recipes and equipment; black "Moon Tower" cards (one per region) plus moon-weed tokens that choke off region decks until you destroy the tower; crescent tokens as a new currency; and — usable in any mode — talent cards (one active ability at a time, swapped on earning a virtue) paired with curse cards as their double-edged downside. There are 14 new birds (a huge boon, since the base game shipped only four) and eight new paupers, plus expanded, more robust solo rules.

The standout is the separate Illustrated Field Guide & Achievements Journal — part lore book, part coloring book, part 500-task tick-list — which he adores as a wonderfully creative companion he'd love to see other games copy.

----

Should a big expansion stay siloed as its own adventure (like Moon Towers) or fold everything into one bigger game — which do you prefer?
